memes.label: Ambition
memes.description: The theme of personal and social ambition in The Getting of Wisdom, driving both Laura Rambotham's aspirations and her Mother's hopes for her.

memes.label: Annie Johns
memes.description: Annie Johns is a schoolmate at the Ladies' College whose public expulsion — formalized in a satirical letter written by Laura — becomes one of the novel's defining moral crises, a moment that haunts Laura's conscience and crystallizes the cruelty latent in school society.

memes.label: Anxiety
memes.description: The pervasive anxiety experienced by Laura Rambotham throughout The Getting of Wisdom as she navigates the treacherous social terrain of school life — a condition that manifests as nervousness, dread, and crippling fear, shaping her behavior and her sense of self from the moment she arrives at the Ladies' College.

memes.label: Australian Identity
memes.description: Australian identity in "The Getting of Wisdom" is an anxious, inchoate formation — a colonial consciousness suspended between English cultural models and Australian realities, where the question of what it means to be Australian is asked obliquely through the daily experience of schoolgirls learning to be "young ladies" in a society that measures itself against a distant imperial center.

memes.label: Ballarat
memes.description: Ballarat is referenced in "The Getting of Wisdom" as the location of a theatrical performance of Hamlet that Laura attended before coming to the Ladies' College — a memory that shapes her understanding of dramatic spectacle.

memes.label: Belonging
memes.description: Laura's quest to belong at the Ladies' College drives much of the novel's action. From her first arrival as an outsider to her final ambiguous acceptance, belonging — and its absence — shapes her moral choices, her friendships, and her sense of self. It is the central emotional engine of "The Getting of Wisdom."

memes.label: Bertha Ramsey
memes.description: Bertha Ramsey is a schoolmate at the Ladies' College, close friend of Inez, known for her ready tears and warm-hearted nature — she represents a particular mode of schoolgirl friendship that Laura alternately participates in and observes from the outside.

memes.label: Bible
memes.description: The Bible is a pervasive presence in the Ladies' College of "The Getting of Wisdom," functioning as an instrument of discipline, a requirement of piety, and — for Laura — an occasion for audacious performance rather than genuine devotion.

memes.label: Boarding School
memes.description: The boarding school in "The Getting of Wisdom" — embodied by the Ladies' College in Melbourne — provides the institutional container for Laura Rambotham's coming-of-age, functioning as both a site of formal education and a hothouse of social learning where class, gender, and character are forged through daily contact with peers and authority.

memes.label: Bob
memes.description: Tilly Macnamara's handsome cousin, approximately nineteen years old, who becomes the object of Laura Rambotham's first romantic interest. A beautiful youth with liquid eyes and blanched-almond teeth, Bob unwittingly teaches Laura about the painful gap between adolescent social expectations and her own natural inclinations.

memes.label: Characters
memes.description: Complete list of characters in The Getting of Wisdom (1910) by Henry Handel Richardson.

memes.label: Childhood
memes.description: Childhood in "The Getting of Wisdom" is both the state Laura Rambotham is leaving behind and the standard against which her subsequent development is measured — a condition of relative freedom, imaginative richness, and authentic selfhood that the novel elegizes even as it chronicles its necessary loss.

memes.label: Laura Chin
memes.description: Laura Chin, known universally as "Chinky," is a schoolmate at the Ladies' College whose nickname — casually racialized and accepted without question — illuminates the unexamined prejudices of late-Victorian colonial society that the novel documents with unsparing clarity.

memes.label: Christmas
memes.description: Christmas in "The Getting of Wisdom" punctuates the school year with visits home — each return marking Laura's increasing distance from her family and her deepening absorption into the social world of the Ladies' College.

memes.label: Church
memes.description: The role of church attendance and religious institutions in The Getting of Wisdom.

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: The Church of England in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the religious establishment that frames the Ladies' College's spiritual life — the institutional context for Sunday observances, Bible classes, clerical authority, and the denominational identity that marks the school's students as "church girls" in the sectarian landscape of colonial Melbourne.

memes.label: Class
memes.description: Social class distinctions and hierarchy at the Ladies' College and in 1890s Melbourne, as experienced by Laura Rambotham — from her modest family background to her navigation of the school's social order.

memes.label: Class Two
memes.description: Class Two at the Ladies' College represents a significant stage in Laura's academic advancement — the form she enters as a reward for her progress, marking her ascent through the school's hierarchical structure.

memes.label: Clothing
memes.description: Clothing in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the most visible currency of social distinction — Laura's homemade dresses, wrong-length skirts, and inadequate wardrobe mark her as an outsider more indelibly than any other sign.

memes.label: Collins Street
memes.description: Collins Street, specifically "the block" — the stretch forming Melbourne's fashionable promenade — is the setting for one of the novel's most vivid social scenes. Here, Laura accompanies Tilly on a Saturday morning shopping expedition, observing the rituals of display, flirtation, and social performance that define Melbourne's late-Victorian public life. The street is a stage where class, gender, and desirability are publicly negotiated.

memes.label: Colonial Identity
memes.description: The colonial Australian identity as experienced by the "young colonials" at the Ladies' College — caught between British cultural norms and emerging Australian self-consciousness in late-nineteenth-century Melbourne.

memes.label: Colonial Identity — Between England and Australia
memes.description: An analysis of the tension between Australian reality and British aspiration that pervades "The Getting of Wisdom" — the colonial condition as it shapes education, culture, and self-understanding at the Ladies' College.

memes.label: Colonial Society
memes.description: Colonial society in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the social world of late-nineteenth-century Melbourne — a hierarchical, Anglophile, and class-conscious milieu in which the Ladies' College girls and their families negotiate status through dress, speech, accomplishment, and the ever-present comparison with "Home" (England).

memes.label: Concepts & Themes
memes.description: Themes, ideas, and motifs explored in The Getting of Wisdom — from music and class to belonging and ambition.

memes.label: Annual Concert
memes.description: The annual concert at the Ladies' College is the culminating performance event of the school year in "The Getting of Wisdom" — the occasion on which Laura Rambotham's musical talent is publicly displayed and recognized, and one of the few moments in the novel when institutional validation aligns with genuine achievement.

memes.label: Conformity
memes.description: Conformity in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the overwhelming social imperative that governs life at the Ladies' College — the pressure to dress, speak, and behave according to unwritten codes whose mastery defines one's place in the school's hierarchy and whose violation courts exclusion.

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: The various forms of courage — moral, social, and desperate — that characters in the novel must summon, particularly Laura Rambotham in her confrontations with truth and social judgment.

memes.label: Cousin Grace
memes.description: Cousin Grace is Laura Rambotham's older cousin who accompanies her from the country to the Ladies' College in Melbourne — her boisterous laughter and worldly knowingness introduce Laura to the intimidating social world she is about to enter alone.

memes.label: Cupid Stanhope
memes.description: Cupid Stanhope is a senior student and prefect at the Ladies' College, a figure of social prestige who co-leads the Literary Society and represents the school's internal aristocracy — the girls whose status derives from family wealth and social connections rather than academic or artistic achievement.

memes.label: Curiosity
memes.description: The tension between intellectual and morbid curiosity as a defining trait of Laura Rambotham's character and a critique of the educational environment at Ladies' College.

memes.label: Death
memes.description: Death in "The Getting of Wisdom" appears as an absent presence — Laura's deceased father haunts the margins of the narrative, while the language of mortality surfaces in moments of emotional intensity, coloring the novel's treatment of loss, longing, and identity.

memes.label: Deception
memes.description: Laura Rambotham's habit of fabrication and storytelling — from childhood tall tales to elaborate deceptions at the Ladies' College that become her primary currency of social acceptance.

memes.label: The Deception Network — Performance, Identity, and Social Survival
memes.description: A cross-entity analysis of how deception, storytelling, and identity performance function as interconnected survival strategies in "The Getting of Wisdom" — from Laura's romantic fictions to the school's own institutional hypocrisies.

memes.label: Dr. Pughson
memes.description: The Headmaster of Ladies' College, a red-eyed, feared figure who sets notoriously difficult examination papers and embodies institutional authority over the students.

memes.label: Dream
memes.description: The role of dreams, dreaming, and nightmares in Laura Rambotham's inner life — from the "sea of dreams" of imaginative escape to the recurring nightmare of expulsion and social persecution.

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: The concept of duty as a moral and institutional force at the Ladies' College — from school rules to filial obligation.

memes.label: East Melbourne
memes.description: East Melbourne is the neighborhood surrounding the Ladies' College in "The Getting of Wisdom," its broad white streets forming the setting for the boarders' daily walks and Laura's first taste of freedom in the city.

memes.label: Eastern Market
memes.description: The Eastern Market, a nineteenth-century Melbourne marketplace referenced in connection with Collins Street and the city's commercial life, appears in "The Getting of Wisdom" as part of the urban geography through which the Ladies' College students navigate the city's social and commercial spaces.

memes.label: Education
memes.description: Education in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the central institutional framework of the novel — both the formal curriculum of the Ladies' College and the less formal but more consequential education in social hierarchy, conformity, and self-suppression that Laura Rambotham undergoes.

memes.label: Elocution
memes.description: Elocution — the art of formal public speaking and recitation — appears in "The Getting of Wisdom" as a curricular subject at the Ladies' College, taught by the visiting master Mr. Repton, and functions as a vehicle for the novel's exploration of performance, class distinction, and the gap between authentic expression and social display.

memes.label: England
memes.description: England appears in "The Getting of Wisdom" as a cultural and religious reference point — the map on the classroom wall, the established Church, and the distant "Mother Country" that shapes colonial Australian education and identity.

memes.label: Erwin
memes.description: Erwin is the Godmother's pug-nosed son in "The Getting of Wisdom," one of the "wild boys" of the Prahran household who terrorises Laura Rambotham during her monthly holiday visit in Chapter VIII.

memes.label: Evelyn Souttar
memes.description: Evelyn Souttar is a beautiful, socially confident student at the Ladies' College who becomes the object of Laura's intense friendship and admiration, representing the allure of social acceptance and its ultimate inability to satisfy Laura's deeper needs.

memes.label: Examinations
memes.description: The public examinations — both the internal test-examinations and the final matriculation examination — form the academic climax of "The Getting of Wisdom," representing the institutional judgment that determines Laura Rambotham's future and crystallizing the novel's ambivalent relationship to formal education.

memes.label: Failure
memes.description: The experience of failure — academic, social, and romantic — as a recurring pattern in Laura Rambotham's school years, and its role in shaping her self-understanding.

memes.label: Family
memes.description: The Tiverton family — Mother, Laura, Pin, Leppie, and siblings — forms the domestic cradle from which Laura departs and the gravitational pull that tugs at her throughout her years at the Ladies' College. Richardson uses the family to contrast the warmth and chaos of home against the rigid order of institutional life.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: The pervasive emotional state that governs behavior at the Ladies' College — fear of authority, fear of social exposure, fear of the future — and shapes Laura Rambotham's interior life throughout her education.

memes.label: Fitzroy Gardens
memes.description: The Fitzroy Gardens are public gardens adjacent to the Ladies' College in "The Getting of Wisdom," their "thick, exotic greenery" forming a distinctive feature of the East Melbourne landscape that Laura encounters on her first walk outside the school grounds.

memes.label: Food
memes.description: Food in "The Getting of Wisdom" is a minor but persistent motif — from the denial of meals as discipline to the social significance of what appears on the table, eating reveals the school's hierarchies as surely as any examination result.

memes.label: Frank Rambotham
memes.description: Frank is Laura Rambotham's youngest brother, one of the siblings who populate the novel's opening chapters — a figure of childhood innocence and vulnerability whose presence anchors Laura's pre-school identity.

memes.label: Friendship
memes.description: Friendship in "The Getting of Wisdom" is a site of intense longing, strategic calculation, and repeated disappointment — the arena in which Laura Rambotham learns that intimacy is governed by the same social hierarchies that structure every other aspect of school life.

memes.label: Garden
memes.description: The gardens of the Ladies' College and the Rambotham home as significant settings in The Getting of Wisdom.

memes.label: Gender
memes.description: Gender expectations and the construction of femininity at Ladies' College, including the "woman's brain" critique and the novel's portrayal of woman as prey.

memes.label: Georgina
memes.description: Georgina — "Georgy" — is the Godmother's elder daughter in "The Getting of Wisdom," a dumpy, brusque young woman whose volatile courtship with Joey provides Laura Rambotham with her first close observation of adult romantic negotiation during a monthly holiday at Prahran.

memes.label: Godmother
memes.description: Laura Rambotham's godmother, a sharp-tongued but generous elderly lady who lives at Prahran and provides crucial support for Laura's education at the Ladies' College, including schoolbooks and holiday accommodation.

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: Guilt permeates Laura Rambotham's inner life at the Ladies' College — the guilt of social errors, of fabrications discovered, of the examination cheating incident, and of her broader failure to meet the moral standards imposed by figures like Mrs. Gurley and Mary Pidwall. Richardson treats guilt not as theological condition but as social weapon.

memes.label: History
memes.description: History in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the subject Laura fails in her matriculation examination — the discipline that most directly exposes the gap between her quick intelligence and her inability to master institutional forms of knowledge.

memes.label: Honesty
memes.description: The moral ideal of truth-telling in The Getting of Wisdom, explored through Laura Rambotham's struggle to reconcile honesty with social survival and creative self-expression.

memes.label: Hope
memes.description: Hope and disappointment as shaping forces in Laura Rambotham's school experience and her mother's aspirations for her.

memes.label: Humor
memes.description: Humor in "The Getting of Wisdom" is primarily the narrator's ironic distance from Laura's experience — a tool of deflation that exposes the gap between Laura's intense subjectivity and the mundane reality of school life.

memes.label: Identity
memes.description: Identity in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the central existential problem Laura Rambotham confronts — the question of who she is, who she wishes to be, and who the social world will permit her to become, negotiated through the competing claims of family origin, artistic talent, peer acceptance, and social aspiration.

memes.label: Imagination
memes.description: Laura Rambotham's rich imaginative life as both a coping mechanism and a moral liability, blurring the line between creativity and deception.

memes.label: Inez
memes.description: Inez is a schoolmate at the Ladies' College, the inseparable chum of Bertha Ramsey, known for her deep slow voice and pretty face — she represents the intimate texture of schoolgirl friendship and the quiet power of social alliance.

memes.label: Innocence
memes.description: The theme of innocence and its gradual loss as Laura Rambotham navigates the social complexities of school life.

memes.label: Irony
memes.description: Irony is the dominant narrative mode of "The Getting of Wisdom" — a sustained ironic distance between the narrator's perception of Laura's experience and Laura's own understanding that generates both the novel's comedy and its pathos, and that registers Richardson's critique of the social world her protagonist must navigate.

memes.label: Isabella Shepherd
memes.description: Miss Isabella Shepherd is a divinity student at Trinity College, sister of Olive Shepherd's — a warm, smiling figure who becomes one of Laura's staunch allies and represents a mode of adult femininity distinct from the school's authority figures, blending piety with genuine human warmth.

memes.label: Jealousy
memes.description: Jealousy and envy as formative emotional experiences in The Getting of Wisdom — impulses that structure the social dynamics of the Ladies' College, drive friendships and rivalries, and reveal the profound insecurity beneath Laura Rambotham's fierce attachment to those she admires.

memes.label: Jim Chapman
memes.description: Jim Chapman is the brother of Miss Chapman, a mistress at the Ladies' College in "The Getting of Wisdom," mentioned across several chapters as part of the extended social network surrounding the school.

memes.label: Joey
memes.description: Joey is Georgina's suitor in "The Getting of Wisdom," a fat, lazy-walking young man whose loud telephone quarrels and reconciliations with Georgina provide Laura Rambotham with her first close observation of adult courtship during the Prahran holiday in Chapter VIII.

memes.label: Kate Horner
memes.description: Kate Horner is a schoolmate at the Ladies' College, a member of Maria Morell's clique known for her outspoken manner and hearty appetite — she represents the rough-and-tumble energy of schoolgirl society, earthy and unselfconscious in a world of careful performances.

memes.label: Kindness
memes.description: Kindness in "The Getting of Wisdom" is a rare and precious commodity — the small acts of generosity that punctuate Laura's school experience and remind us that the Ladies' College is not wholly defined by its cruelties.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: Formal learning and book-knowledge as distinct from wisdom in the novel — the curriculum and examination system of the Ladies' College, and the novel's sustained argument that institutional knowledge alone is insufficient for life.

memes.label: Ladies' College
memes.description: The Ladies' College is the central institutional setting of "The Getting of Wisdom" — an elite private boarding school for girls in East Melbourne where Laura Rambotham receives her formal education and her more painful education in social hierarchy and conformity.

memes.label: Latin
memes.description: Latin in "The Getting of Wisdom" functions as a marker of academic advancement and social distinction — Laura's ignorance of it reveals her educational disadvantages, while her eventual study of it signals her ascent through the school's curriculum.

memes.label: Laughter
memes.description: The social functions of laughter in The Getting of Wisdom — as bonding mechanism, weapon of exclusion, and marker of belonging.

memes.label: Laura Rambotham
memes.description: Laura Rambotham is the protagonist of "The Getting of Wisdom," a country girl from rural Victoria sent to an elite Melbourne ladies' college where she navigates the tensions between her upbringing, her musical talent, and the rigid social expectations of colonial society.

memes.label: Leppie
memes.description: Leppie is Laura Rambotham's middle younger brother in "The Getting of Wisdom," appearing in the novel's opening chapters as part of the sibling constellation that defines Laura's pre-school identity — his curious, questioning nature provides a distinct voice among the Rambotham children before Laura departs for Melbourne.

memes.label: Lessons
memes.description: The formal lessons and curriculum at the Ladies' College in The Getting of Wisdom, and the tension between academic instruction and the informal "lessons" of social life.

memes.label: Letters
memes.description: The role of letters and written correspondence in The Getting of Wisdom as vehicles for communication between school and home.

memes.label: Lilith Gordon
memes.description: Lilith Gordon is Laura Rambotham's first roommate at the Ladies' College, a girl of superior social standing whose casual cruelty and class condescension make her the catalyst for Laura's earliest lessons in schoolyard social politics.

memes.label: Literary Society
memes.description: The Literary Society is a selective student organization at the Ladies' College in "The Getting of Wisdom," membership in which serves as a marker of social and academic prestige among the boarders and provides the setting for Laura Rambotham's fateful encounter with Evelyn Souttar.

memes.label: Lolo
memes.description: Lolo is one of Laura Rambotham's schoolmates at the Ladies' College in "The Getting of Wisdom," described as "a merry little lump of a girl" who "darted her head from side to side when she spoke, with the movements of a watchful bird."

memes.label: Loneliness
memes.description: Laura's experience of loneliness — as a newcomer, as an outsider among peers, and as a "bottled-up" child isolated by her own sensitivity and secrecy.

memes.label: Lucy
memes.description: Lucy of Toorak is a saucy young student at the Ladies' College whose sharp wit and social confidence make her both admired and feared by her classmates — she represents the cutting edge of schoolgirl society, where verbal skill confers real power.

memes.label: Maisie Shepherd
memes.description: Maisie Shepherd — Mrs. Shepherd — is the wife of the curate Robby Shepherd and sister-in-law to Isabella Shepherd. An older, tired-faced woman married to a younger man, she manages the household in Chapter XVI during Laura's visit, enduring her husband's petulant demands and her sister-in-law's corrective admonishments with a gentle, ineffectual dignity.

memes.label: Manners
memes.description: The role of manners and social etiquette in The Getting of Wisdom as markers of class and breeding.

memes.label: Maria Morell
memes.description: Maria Morell is a schoolmate at the Ladies' College, known as "the fat girl", who oscillates between defending Laura from teasing and participating in the social dynamics that exclude her — a figure who embodies the complex, shifting moral landscape of school society.

memes.label: Marina
memes.description: Godmother's younger daughter, a short, sleek-haired young woman of about twenty who supplies Laura Rambotham with hand-me-down schoolbooks and serves as her escort for monthly holidays at Prahran. Possesses her mother's brisk, matter-of-fact manner.

memes.label: Marmaduke
memes.description: Marmaduke is one of the Godmother's sons in "The Getting of Wisdom," who together with his brother Erwin terrorises Laura Rambotham during her holiday visit — memorably mocking her purple dress as "the purple parrot."

memes.label: Mary Pidwall
memes.description: A senior student at Ladies' College, sixteen years old, whose intelligence and moral seriousness make her an intimidating figure to younger students including Laura Rambotham.

memes.label: Mary
memes.description: Mary is a maid at the Ladies' College in "The Getting of Wisdom," a recurring domestic presence remembered for her "pleasant smile" who appears across multiple chapters as part of the institutional background against which Laura Rambotham's school life unfolds.

memes.label: Melbourne
memes.description: Melbourne in the 1890s serves as the metropolitan setting of "The Getting of Wisdom," representing the world of colonial respectability, social aspiration, and institutional education into which Laura Rambotham is thrust from her rural home.

memes.label: Miss Blount
memes.description: Miss Blount is the secretary at the Ladies' College, whose dramatic entrance with evidence of examination cheating sets in motion the Annie Johns scandal in Chapter XII — she represents the institutional conscience caught between duty and human sympathy.

memes.label: Miss Chapman
memes.description: Miss Chapman is the head governess at the Ladies' College in "The Getting of Wisdom," a figure of institutional authority whose musical discernment and stern propriety shape much of Laura's school experience.

memes.label: Miss Day
memes.description: Miss Day is a teacher or governess at the Ladies' College, characterized by her stiff black hair and lynx-eyed vigilance over student conduct — a figure who embodies the institutional authority that both constrains and shapes Laura's school experience.

memes.label: Miss Hicks
memes.description: Visiting governess for geography at Ladies' College whose sharp critique of Inez sparked Laura Rambotham's intellectual awakening.

memes.label: Miss Snodgrass
memes.description: Miss Snodgrass is an assistant governess at the Ladies' College, one of the subordinate figures in the school's chain of female authority whose presence contributes to the novel's portrait of the institutional apparatus that shapes, judges, and constrains Laura Rambotham.

memes.label: Morality
memes.description: The moral framework of the Ladies' College — its codes, enforcement through public shaming and peer judgment, and the gap between institutional morality and Laura Rambotham's adolescent experience.

memes.label: Mother
memes.description: Laura Rambotham's mother — known to her children as "Pin" — is the widowed country woman whose love for her daughter is complicated by her social inadequacy, her needlework occupation, and her inability to prepare Laura for the world of the Ladies' College.

memes.label: Mr. Repton
memes.description: Visiting elocution master at the Ladies' College, rumoured former actor, admired by students for his handsome legs.

memes.label: Mr. Shepherd
memes.description: Mr. Shepherd is the Anglican clergyman father of Isabella, Maisie, and Olive Shepherd, whose household Laura visits in Chapter XVI — a scene that exposes the contrast between Laura's fabricated social identity and the domestic reality of a respectable Melbourne family.

memes.label: Mr. Strachey
memes.description: Co-administrator of Ladies' College alongside Mrs. Gurley, an imposing figure over six feet tall who handles student complaints, discipline, and social events with an awkward formality.

memes.label: Mrs. Gurley
memes.description: Mrs. Gurley is the Lady Superintendent of the Ladies' College, a formidable figure of institutional authority whose chilly demeanor and strict surveillance represent the repressive social order that Laura Rambotham must navigate.

memes.label: Mrs. Shepherd
memes.description: Mrs. Shepherd is the anxious, hospitable wife of the Reverend Mr. Shepherd and mother of three girls at the Ladies' College, whose nervous management of Laura's monthly-holiday visit in Chapter XVI provides one of the novel's most vivid portraits of Victorian maternal domesticity.

memes.label: Mrs. Strachey
memes.description: Wife of Mr. Strachey, co-administrator of the Ladies' College. Mrs. Strachey hosts drawing-room social events for selected boarders and oversees musical performances, serving as a gentler counterpoint to Mrs. Gurley's stern discipline.

memes.label: Music
memes.description: The role of music and musical performance in the social and educational world of Laura Rambotham at her Melbourne boarding school.

memes.label: Music as Identity — Laura Rambotham's Talent and Its Price
memes.description: An analysis of how Laura Rambotham's musical talent functions as her sole source of distinction at the Ladies' College — a gift that simultaneously elevates and isolates her, offering recognition on the institution's terms while extracting a cost she cannot begin to calculate.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: Nature in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the world outside the Ladies' College — the garden, the countryside, the outdoor spaces that represent freedom and authenticity in contrast to the school's regulated interiors.

memes.label: Nelly Bristow
memes.description: A student at the Ladies' College noted for a peculiar vocal mannerism — small bubbles from her mouth when speaking.

memes.label: Nietzsche Epigraph
memes.description: Several chapters of "The Getting of Wisdom" open with epigraphs from Friedrich Nietzsche's works, linking the novel's themes of self-overcoming, social morality, and individual will to the philosopher's ideas and transforming a domestic school story into a philosophical inquiry.

memes.label: Old Anne
memes.description: Old Anne is a servant in the Rambotham household — one of the domestic figures who populate Laura's pre-school world and whose presence marks the family's precarious claim to middle-class respectability.

memes.label: Olive Shepherd
memes.description: Olive Shepherd is a schoolmate at the Ladies' College whose elder sister Isabella Shepherd, a divinity student at Trinity College, becomes one of Laura Rambotham's warmest adult allies and introduces Laura to a wider intellectual world beyond the school's confines.

memes.label: Oliver Cromwell
memes.description: Oliver Cromwell appears in "The Getting of Wisdom" as a figure of historical study — one of the subjects Laura encounters in the school curriculum, representing the British history that colonial education was designed to transmit.

memes.label: Ormond College
memes.description: Ormond College, the Presbyterian residential college of the University of Melbourne, appears alongside Trinity College in "The Getting of Wisdom" as part of the masculine university world that represents the intellectual horizon beyond the Ladies' College's limited ambitions for its students.

memes.label: Patrick O'Donnell
memes.description: Patrick O'Donnell is the Irish-Australian coach driver who transports Laura Rambotham from her rural home to the railway station in the opening chapters of "The Getting of Wisdom" — a warm, comic figure whose kindness provides a transitional buffer between the warmth of Laura's family and the institutional coldness awaiting her in Melbourne.

memes.label: Peter
memes.description: Peter is a family acquaintance encountered at the railway station as Laura departs for Melbourne — a yellow-haired boy whose awkward questions and embarrassed curiosity capture the provincial world Laura is leaving behind.

memes.label: Piano
memes.description: The role of the piano and pianistic skill in The Getting of Wisdom as Laura Rambotham's primary talent and vehicle for social recognition.

memes.label: Pin
memes.description: Laura Rambotham's younger sibling, nicknamed "Pin," known for tearfulness and the source of the childhood dynamics in the opening chapters of the novel.

memes.label: Places
memes.description: All locations referenced in The Getting of Wisdom — Melbourne, its suburbs, and beyond.

memes.label: Prahran
memes.description: Prahran is a Melbourne suburb where Laura's godmother lives, serving as Laura's refuge from the Ladies' College during weekend visits in "The Getting of Wisdom."

memes.label: Prayer
memes.description: The role of prayer and private religious devotion in The Getting of Wisdom.

memes.label: Pride
memes.description: "Pride is Laura Rambotham's defining trait and her most persistent vulnerability. It drives her achievements — musical, academic, literary — but also isolates her from peers who resent her unwillingness to conceal her abilities. Richardson traces pride through its full arc: family pride, wounded pride, artistic pride, and the hard-won pride of self-knowledge."

memes.label: Punishment
memes.description: Systems and instances of punishment at the Ladies' College in The Getting of Wisdom — the formal and informal mechanisms by which authority enforces conformity, from the rebukes of Mrs. Gurley to the ultimate sanction of expulsion, and the moral lessons these punishments are meant to teach.

memes.label: Reading
memes.description: Reading in "The Getting of Wisdom" is both a marker of Laura's intellectual vitality and a site of social regulation — what one reads, when, and how reveals the school's anxious control over the imaginative lives of its students.

memes.label: Rebellion
memes.description: Rebellion in "The Getting of Wisdom" is Laura Rambotham's intermittent and ambivalent response to the social pressures of the Ladies' College — a series of small acts of defiance (the fabricated stories, the withheld truths, the moments of refusal) that mark the survival of a self unwilling to be entirely remade by institutional demands.

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: Religion at the Ladies' College is an institutional routine rather than a spiritual practice — daily prayers, Sunday church attendance, hymn-singing, and Bible verse memorization form the framework of school discipline. Richardson presents religion primarily as an instrument of social control, embodied in Mrs. Gurley's evangelical severity and the empty rituals of compulsory worship.

memes.label: Rivalry
memes.description: Academic and social rivalry among students at the Ladies' College in The Getting of Wisdom.

memes.label: River
memes.description: River imagery and waterside settings in The Getting of Wisdom, including the Yarra River and childhood associations with water.

memes.label: Romance
memes.description: "Romance in \"The Getting of Wisdom\" operates on multiple levels: the schoolgirl culture of crushes and admirers, Laura's infatuation with Bob, the romantic fiction she writes for the Literary Society, and the broader question of whether romance offers liberation or merely a different kind of confinement. Richardson treats romance with characteristic irony — as both genuine emotional experience and social performance."

memes.label: Rules
memes.description: The system of rules and regulations governing student life at the Ladies' College in The Getting of Wisdom — an intricate architecture of control that shapes every dimension of the girls' experience and serves as the backdrop against which Laura Rambotham learns the costs of compliance and transgression.

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: The financial and emotional sacrifices made by Mother to fund Laura Rambotham's education, and Laura's gradual awakening to the cost of her schooling.

memes.label: Sandy
memes.description: Sandy is a young man mentioned in connection with the Chapman family in "The Getting of Wisdom," appearing briefly across several chapters as part of the school's extended social network.

memes.label: Sarah
memes.description: The maidservant of the Rambotham household, a busy, plain-spoken figure whose presence shapes the domestic world Laura leaves behind in the opening chapters.

memes.label: Secret
memes.description: The motif of secrets and secret-telling as a central moral and social dynamic in the world of Ladies' College, driving both intimacy and betrayal among the students.

memes.label: Servants
memes.description: Domestic servants in "The Getting of Wisdom" — from Mary the maid-of-all-work at the Ladies' College to Old Anne at Warrenega and the unnamed servants of Melbourne households — form an invisible substratum of labor that both sustains and exposes the class anxieties of the novel's characters.

memes.label: Shame
memes.description: Shame is the dominant emotional register of "The Getting of Wisdom" — the corrosive consciousness of social inadequacy that drives Laura Rambotham's education in the codes of colonial respectability and structures the novel's most painful scenes of self-exposure.

memes.label: Social Class, Education, and Shame — A Thematic Chain
memes.description: A cross-entity analysis of how social class stratification, the institutional education system, and the emotion of shame form an interlocking thematic chain in "The Getting of Wisdom" — each element reinforcing the next to create the novel's central mechanism of social control.

memes.label: Social Expectations
memes.description: The Ladies' College is governed by an intricate web of social expectations — unwritten rules about class, gender, behavior, and belonging that Laura must navigate without instruction. Richardson anatomizes Melbourne's late-Victorian social order through the microcosm of the school, exposing the codes that determine who rises and who is cast out.

memes.label: Social Standing
memes.description: The social hierarchy operating within the Ladies' College and colonial Melbourne, determining status and belonging.

memes.label: Sunday
memes.description: Sunday in "The Getting of Wisdom" is the day when the school's religious machinery is most visible — a regimented interval of church attendance, supervised leisure, and enforced piety that Laura navigates with characteristic restlessness.

memes.label: Sydney
memes.description: Sydney is mentioned as a passing reference in "The Getting of Wisdom" — the destination of the English mail steamer that Laura watches from the beach, a symbol of the wider world from which she feels excluded.

memes.label: Teasing
memes.description: The culture of teasing at the Ladies' College and beyond — from casual classroom banter to the menacing male tease — as a mechanism of social control and humiliation in the world of the novel.

memes.label: Tilly
memes.description: Tilly is a schoolmate of Laura Rambotham at the Ladies' College, one of the few girls who treats Laura with consistent kindness and whose cousin Bob becomes the catalyst for one of Laura's most painful social humiliations.

memes.label: Tilly's Uncle
memes.description: Tilly's Uncle is a central comic-antagonistic figure of Chapter XIV in "The Getting of Wisdom," an eccentric family elder whose catchphrase "let's call in the cats!" and overbearing presence dominate Laura Rambotham's holiday visit to Tilly's home.

memes.label: Toorak
memes.description: Toorak is an affluent Melbourne suburb in "The Getting of Wisdom," associated with the character Lucy and serving as a marker of the highest social standing in the novel's school hierarchy.

memes.label: Tradition
memes.description: The role of tradition and inherited custom in shaping the world of the Ladies' College and colonial Melbourne society.

memes.label: Trinity College
memes.description: Trinity College, the University of Melbourne residential college, appears in "The Getting of Wisdom" as the symbol of elite masculine education and the aspirational horizon toward which the novel's most accomplished female characters direct their ambitions.

memes.label: Uncle Tom
memes.description: Uncle Tom is mentioned briefly in "The Getting of Wisdom" as a family relation of Laura's — one of the Rambotham connections that anchor her in the provincial world she is leaving behind.

memes.label: University of Melbourne
memes.description: The University of Melbourne, though never directly depicted in "The Getting of Wisdom," functions as the absent center of the novel's educational hierarchy — the institution whose colleges (Trinity, Ormond) and degree programs represent genuine intellectual life beyond the "accomplishments" curriculum of the Ladies' College.

memes.label: Vanity
memes.description: Personal vanity and concern with physical appearance as markers of social standing among the students of the Ladies' College — and Laura Rambotham's ambivalent relationship with her own looks.

memes.label: Vaucluse
memes.description: Vaucluse is Bertha Ramsey's family home in "The Getting of Wisdom" — a grand residence whose name carries class connotations, and the site of a "shameful secret" about Bertha's mother that circulates among the schoolgirls.

memes.label: Warrenega
memes.description: Warrenega is Laura Rambotham's rural hometown in "The Getting of Wisdom," the place she leaves to attend school in Melbourne and to which her family ties and childhood memories remain anchored.

memes.label: Wealth
memes.description: The role of wealth and class distinction in the social hierarchy of Ladies' College and colonial Melbourne society.

memes.label: Wisdom
memes.description: The central thematic concept of the novel — the "wisdom" that Laura Rambotham is said to "get" through her education, and the novel's skeptical interrogation of what that wisdom actually consists of.

memes.label: Writing
memes.description: Writing in "The Getting of Wisdom" is an act of self-presentation fraught with social risk — Laura's letters, compositions, and even her handwriting become sites where her identity is judged, corrected, and occasionally celebrated.

memes.label: Miss Zielinski
memes.description: Miss Zielinski is the governess at the Ladies' College who supervises the younger students — sharp-tongued and observant, she plays a key role in discovering Laura's musical talent during the early chapters of the novel.
